coredata.dc:title: A hybrid optical strategy based on graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles for selective determination of gentamicin in the milk and egg samples
coredata.dc:creator: Sajwan, Reena K.
Solanki, Pratima R.
coredata.dc:description: A rapid and sensitive hybrid nanosensor was reported with a colorimetric and fluorometric readout system using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the detection of gentamicin (GENTA) in milk and egg samples. In the presence of AuNPs, the fluorescence (FL) intensity of GQDs gradually decreased due to the inner filter effect (IFE) of AuNPs on the FL intensity of GQDs. In addition of GENTA into AuNPs, AuNPs get aggregated and changed their color from red to blue, due to which the IFE of AuNPs reduced and hence, enhanced FL intensity of GQDs with varying concentration of GENTA from 1.03 to 16.55 μM with a lower detection limit of 0.422 μM and 0.493 μM in colorimetric and fluorimetric systems, respectively. The practical application of the developed nanosensor is tested against the real spiked sample. The excellent and straightforward recovery efficiency of the developed nanosensor for the determination of GENTA and can be used as a promising and adaptable tool for speedy monitoring of food quantity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles graphene quantum dots
Colorimetric
Fluorometric
Gentamicin

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst otherkwds primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Protocol for the detection of GENTA
Real milk and egg food samples analysis
Influence of AuNPs concentration on fluorescence intensity of GQDs
Optimization of experimental condition for the determination of GENTA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HR-TEM images of GQDs (A, B) and AuNPs (C, D) with inset Figure represent the particle size distribution and interplanar distance of GQDs and AuNPs, respectively.

Absorption spectrum of GQDs (a) and AuNPs (b) with FL emission (c) and excitation (d) spectra of GQDs (A). The FL emission spectrum of GQDs was recorded at different excitation wavelengths varying from 300 to 390 nm (B).

Spectral overlapping between emission spectra of GQDs and absorption spectra of AuNPs to form IFE system (A). Decay life-time of (a) GQDs, (b) GQDs + AuNPs, and (c) Instrument response function (IRF) (B). Influence of AuNPs varying concentrations on the FL intensity of GQDS (C) with intensity (GQDs) versus concentration (AuNPs) plot (D).

FL emission spectra of GQDs in the presence and absence of AuNPs and GENTA, respectively (A). The absorption spectrum of AuNPs is shown in the presence and absence of GENTA (B). TEM images of AuNPs in the presence of GENTA (C) and colorimetric visualization of the color change of AuNPs in the presence of a different concentration of GENTA (D).

UV–Vis absorption spectra of AuNPs in the presence of varying concentrations of GENTA (A). Calibration plot of A678/A524 versus concentrations of GENTA with (B) inset linear relationship plot. The FL intensity of GQDs is shown in the presence of a different concentrations of GENTA (C). The calibration plot between recovery efficiency (%) and different GENTA concentrations with inset Figure represent the linear relationship between them (D).
Selectivity analysis of hybrid nanosensor in colorimetric (A) and fluorometric (B) readout method, respectively.
Systematic representation of hybrid optical nanosensor.

head.ce:title: A hybrid optical strategy based on graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles for selective determination of gentamicin in the milk and egg samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Special Centre for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Special Centre for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rapid and sensitive hybrid nanosensor was reported with a colorimetric and fluorometric readout system using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the detection of gentamicin (GENTA) in milk and egg samples. In the presence of AuNPs, the fluorescence (FL) intensity of GQDs gradually decreased due to the inner filter effect (IFE) of AuNPs on the FL intensity of GQDs. In addition of GENTA into AuNPs, AuNPs get aggregated and changed their color from red to blue, due to which the IFE of AuNPs reduced and hence, enhanced FL intensity of GQDs with varying concentration of GENTA from 1.03 to 16.55 μM with a lower detection limit of 0.422 μM and 0.493 μM in colorimetric and fluorimetric systems, respectively. The practical application of the developed nanosensor is tested against the real spiked sample. The excellent and straightforward recovery efficiency of the developed nanosensor for the determination of GENTA and can be used as a promising and adaptable tool for speedy monitoring of food quantity.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Food Safety and Standards Authority of India

ce:section.ce:para: The uncontrolled use of antibiotics in humans and animals is the main reason behind the abandoned growth of antibacterial resistance. This problem caused a major threat to the public as well as animal health. So that it is most important to develop a technique that can detect the evidence of antibiotics in real food and water samples, and can be successfully used for the safety of food and public health. Gentamicin (GENTA) is a broad-spectrum aminoglycosides antibiotics produced by fermentation of Micromonospora purpura and essentially stops the growth of a wide range of gram-positive and negative bacteria such as Serratia marcescens and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Schmid, 2016; Weinstein, Luedemann, Oden, & Wagman, 1963). GENTA is most commonly used in humans and veterinary to treat mastitis, urinary infection, pneumonia, sepsis in cattle [1]. Due to the unnecessary used of GENTA to treat swine, and poultry for mass production and infection in cattle, there is a possibility that the antibiotics residue could be entered into the foods produced from these animals (Gukowsky, Tan, Han, & He, 2018). The existence of antibiotics residue in foods can potentially cause a hazard to human health. Thus, it is important to develop a system that can detect the presence of these antibiotics in food samples and can be successfully used for food and human health safety. The food safety and standards authority of India (FSSAI) has set the highest residue limit of antibiotics as 0.001 mg kg−1 in foods (Jothi, Joshi, Vijay, Muthuvinayagam, & Sagayaraj, 2013).

The various analytical methods such as immunoassays (Jin, Jang, Han, & Lee, 2005), liquid chromatography (LC) (Adams, Roelants, De Paepe, Roets, & Hoogmartens, 1998), gas–liquid chromatography (G-LC) (Mayhew & Gorbach, 1978), and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Clarot, Chaimbault, Hasdenteufel, Netter, & Nicolas, 2004), were used for the determination of GENTA in food samples. Although these technologies are susceptible, they are not as adaptable in daily food safety monitoring due to their complexity and time-consuming factors. Thus, some reliable, fast, and cost-effective techniques are required to detect GENTA because of their superiority, such as rapid detection and high selectivity applications. Among the different detection techniques, the optical technique in which colorimetric and fluorometric readout detection methods are the most convenient and most accessible techniques for all. Colorimetric readout system is the qualitative analysis of the analytes. It has the advantage of visual detection of analytes through the naked eyes.

Fluorescence (FL) is one of the commonly used optical techniques, due to its simple and easy operational setup, good sensitivity. The fluorescence spectroscopy has well-understood principles behind FL quenching mechanisms such as dynamic quenching and static quenching, Photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and inner filter effect (IFE) (Mehlhorn, Rahimi, & Joseph, 2018) make the technique more suitable for the analysis.

Inner filter effect (IFE) is one of the quenching mechanisms in FL spectroscopy techniques. IFE is the phenomenon that occurred when the absorption spectra of quencher/acceptor overlapped either with emission and/or excitation spectra of donor molecules and form donor–acceptor pairs (Zu, Yan, Bai, Xu, Wang, Huang, et al., 2017). With the excitation wavelength, donor molecules excited and go to the higher energy state, after a while when it returns down to the lower energy (ground) state emit the additional energy in the form of light photons. The light emitted by the donor is then absorbed by the quencher/acceptor molecules and reduced the FL intensity of donor molecules. For the development of IFE based system, a fluorescent donor and an efficient absorber are required. With the recent developments in the fluorescent-based system carbon-based quantum dots and metal NPs, were serves wide to form a donor–acceptor pair.

From the recently reported carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are an attractive form of carbon nanomaterials (Shi, Chan, Pang, Ye, Tian, Lyu, et al., 2015) due to their favorable biocompatibility, brilliant fluorescent properties such as high luminescence, good photo stability, life-time, and UV absorption due to the confinement in size (Ritter & Lyding, 2009). The plenty of carboxyl and hydroxyl surface groups of GQDs offers the viability of functional modification and helps in the deployment of GQDs based sensors in which GQDs can act as electron acceptors as well as electron donors (Mehta, Bhardwaj, Bhardwaj, Tuteja, Vinayak, Paul, et al., 2017; Zhou, Zhao, Wang, Yang, Xie, & Fei, 2020). Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) also get enhanced optical properties, dependent on the distance between the particles, and are excellently used as FL quenchers (Zeng, Zhang, Liu, Tu, Kong, & Zhang, 2012). In past reports, AuNPs were used as colorimetric receptors and energy acceptors. AuNPs possess a high extinction coefficient (2.7 * 108 cm−1 M−1) and the broad visible region absorption is efficiently overlapped with the FL emission spectra of energy donors (Oh, Hong, Lee, Nam, Yoon, & Kim, 2005). With the good spectral overlapping and excellent extinction coefficient, AuNPs can be used as a colorimetric indicator in the colorimetric readout system, as well as an effective quencher in the construction of IFE based fluorometric system. Gukowsky et al., used cysteamine-modified AuNPs for the colorimetric detection of GENTA based on the colorimetric distance-dependent theory. Some GQDs-AuNPs dependent FRET system has been reported in which GQDs serve as an energy donor and AuNPs serve as acceptors. The developed system has been used to detect cysteine (Hai, Lin, Chen, & Wang, 2018) and bacteria (Shi et al., 2015). But for the first time, we have reported the GQDs-AuNPs based colorimetric as well as fluorometric hybrid read out system for the detection of GENTA based on the distance dependent and IFE based mechanism. Some of the reporters used GQDs as a reducing as well as the stabilizing agent for the synthesis on AuNPs to form GQDs-AuNPs nanocomposites (Wadhwa, John, Mathur, Khanuja, Bhattacharya, Roy, et al., 2020).

In the present work, a hybrid nanosensor with a colorimetric and fluorometric “Off-On” readout system was reported for the determination of GENTA in food samples. The present detection system has AuNPs and GQDs, where GQDs act as a fluorometric reporter and FL donor, and AuNPs can be used for dual purposes as colorimetric and FL acceptors. Here, the sensing mechanism for GENTA determination depends on the distance and IFE between the two molecules. When a colloidal solution of AuNPs is added to the GQDs they formed an IFE system, and the FL intensity of GQDs decreased due to the absorption of emission photon of GQDs by AuNPs caused by IFE. Upon addition of GENTA into the AuNPs before GQDs, GENTA molecules bind to the surface of AuNPs through Au-N bond formation and change its color from red to purple-blue, which shows that the AuNPs get agglomerated in the presence of GENTA molecules. After 30 min of incubation, the practical solution was centrifuged at 5400 rpm for 2 min, and then the FL emission of the IFE system was captured in the presence of GENTA. After the centrifugation, the agglomerated AuNPs settle down and eliminated the IFE of AuNPs, and more GQDs were freely available in the solution. The FL intensity of the nanosensor enhanced proportionally with the varying concentration of GENTA. The hybrid nanosensor shows the lower limit of detection (LOD) of 0.422 μM and 0.493 μM in colorimetric and fluorimetric readout methods, respectively. The applicability results of the developed hybrid nanosensor in food samples proven that the proposed system shows good selectivity and sensitivity for GENTA and makes it a more promising tool for food safety application.

Gentamicin sulfates (GENTA) (C21H43N5O7H2SO4), norfloxacin (Nor) (C16H18FN3O3), levofloxacin (Levo) (C18H20FN3O4), ofloxacin (Oflo) (C18H20FN3O4), ciprofloxacin (Cipro), Hydrochloride (C17H18FN3O4HCl), florfenicol (Flor) (C12H14Cl2FNO4S), erythromycin (Erythro) (C37H67NO13), thiamphenicol (Thiam) (C12H15Cl2NO5S), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (N2O6Zn6H2O), sodium hydroxide pellets (NaOH), uric acid (UA) (C5H4N4O3), cholesterol (Cho) (C27H46O), arginine (Arg) (C6H14N4O2), ascorbic acid (AA) (C6H8O6), methionine (Met) (C5H11NO2S), casein (C81 H125N22O39P) and urea (CH4N2O) were purchased from SRL. Gold (III) chloride hydrate (HAuCl4), moxifloxacin (MOXI) hydrochloride (C21H24FN3O4), citric acid (CA) (C6H8O7), bovine serum albumin (BSA), and l-cysteine (Cys) (C3H7NO2S) were purchased from Sigma- Aldrich. Ltd. Aspartic acid (Aspt) (C4H7NO4) and folic acid (FA) (C19H19N2O6) were brought from CDH. Magnesium chloride (MgCl2), cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2·H2O), lead chloride (PbCl2), and were purchased from Thomas Baker. Tetracycline (Tet) hydrochloride (C22H24N2O6.HCl), ferrous chloride (FeCl2·H2O), copper chloride (CuCl2), ampicillin (Amphi) sodium salt (C16H18N3O4SNa), and calcium chloride (CaCl2) were obtained from Hi media laboratory Pvt. Ltd. Vitamin D2 and D3 were purchased from Cayman chemical company, USA. Glucose (Glu) (C6H12O6), and sodium chloride (NaCl), and potassium chloride (KCl) were purchased from fine Chem Limited. All the chemicals were utilized without any further purification, and distilled (DI) water was used for the solution preparation.

The particle size and surface morphology of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were analyzed with high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) JEOL JEM-2200FS, Japan instrument. TEM grid was prepared by putting 10–15 μL of GQDs and AuNPs colloidal solution on top of the carbon-coated copper grid kept it for drying overnight. For spectroscopic Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy analysis was performed to monitor the functional group with the universal ATR of Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two instruments. For this analysis, 20–25 μL of colloidal solution of GQDs and AuNPs were put on a diamond crystal, and record the -spectra were up to the wavenumbers scanning range of 400–4000 cm−1. T90 + UV/VIS Spectrophotometer took the absorption spectrum, and the FL spectrum was recorded using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (G9800A). The UV–Vis absorption and FL intensity spectra of colloidal solution of GQDs and AuNPs were recorded by taking the appropriate amount of the solution in a 1 cm cuvette and then simultaneously record the spectra. FL intensity spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 345 nm. The maxima of the spectrum are centered at 416 nm, and kept slit width of excitation and emission fixed at 5 nm for all FL measurements. The centrifuge and pH measurement were done with MINIMAX5400 mini centrifuge and μ pH system 362, respectively. The UV FL analysis cabinet was used for the visual analysis of the prepared samples. The FL life-time measurement of GQDs was performed in absence and presence of AuNPs with the time correlating single photon counting (TCSPC) experimental setup FL-920 (Edinburg Instruments).

Synthesis of GQDs was carried out using the previously described pyrolysis method with slight modification (Mehrzad-Samarin, Faridbod, Dezfuli, & Ganjali, 2017). Briefly, for the preparation of GQDs, citric acid (2 g) and l-cysteine (50 mg) were taken in a 50 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C for 3 min, which was transferred into soft yellow color liquid. Continue the heat treatment for another 30 min duration which changes the solution to deeper orange color. This solution was drop-wise poured into a newly prepared NaOH (100 mL, 10 mg mL−1) under continuous stirring conditions. The prepared solution was then neutralized to pH 7.0 using HCl (1 M) solution.

AuNPs were synthesized by the citrate reduction of HAuCl4, reported in our previous works (Sajwan, Lakshmi, & Solanki, 2020). Usually, a solution of HAuCl4 (1 mM) was heated until it starts boiling under vigorous stirring. Then trisodium citrate (38.8 mM) as a reducing and capping agent was added in the above solution. After the addition of the trisodium citrate, the color of the solution changes yellow to wine red which indicates the synthesis of AuNPs. Further, reaction solution was kept in the same condition for 30 min. Finally, the temperature of the synthesized AuNPs solution was bringing down to the room temperature (25 °C) and, for further use, kept the solution at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protocol for the detection of GENTA
ce:section.ce:para: For the colorimetric and fluorometric detection of GENTA, serial dilutions of different concentrations (1.03, 2.06, 3.10, 4.14, 5.17, 6.21, 7.24, 8.27, 9.31, 10.34, 11.37, 12.41, 13.45, 14.48, 15.51, and 16.55 µM) of GENTA were prepared in DI water. For colorimetric detection of GENTA, 50 µL of different GENTA concentrations were incubated together with 0.5 mL of AuNPs (8.02 nM) in sequence of 2 mL eppendorf tubes for 30 min and then recorded the absorption spectra of AuNPs. For fluorometric determination, firstly, the stock solution of GQDs was diluted about 50-fold (FD) in DI and used a similar composition all through the experiments. For fluorometric detection of GENTA, increasing concentrations of GENTA were added to AuNPs (0.5 mL, 8.02 nM) in sequence of 2 mL eppendorf tubes and kept for 15 min incubation. After that, 40 µL GQDs was added and kept for further incubation. After 30 min of incubation, the reaction mixture was centrifuged for 2 min, and then FL spectra of GQDs-AuNPs based hybrid nanosensor were recorded at a 345 nm excitation wavelength. The experimental values of all the parameters were kept the same all through the detection procedures.

The selectivity analysis of the developed hybrid nanosensor was done with real food samples. For that, all the interferents which are part of the real samples like antibiotics (AB) (Genta, Erythro, Oflo, Amphi, Thiam, Nor, Levo, Tet, Flor, and Cipro), metal ions (MI) (Cd2+, Na+, Zn2+, K+, Ca+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, and Fe2+) and metabolites (MB) (CA, Asp, Glu, Met, Arg, BSA, AA, UA, FA, Cys urea, casein, Cho Vit-D2, and Vit-D3) were elected for the selectivity analysis of the purposed system. The concentration of AB, MB, and MI, interferents was set as 40 µM, 5 mM, and 10 µM, respectively. For interferents study, similar protocols have followed which were performed at the time of detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real milk and egg food samples analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of GENTA concentration in milk and egg samples was determined to evaluate the practicality of the developed system. The milk and chicken egg samples were brought from the local market and store at temperature of 4 °C before use.

Parallel Assay: For performing the parallel assay of milk, five different fold diluted milk samples such as 5-FD, 10-FD, 20-FD, 50-FD, and 100-FD have been taken and tested with the developed nanosensor. Further, all five samples were spiked with the 10.34 μM of GENTA concentration and tested with the nanosensor by recording the absorbance spectra of AuNPs and FL spectra of the GQDs-AuNPs system in the presence of spiked milk samples and calculate their recovery percentage. By evaluating the dilution factor, further 100-FD diluted milk samples have taken and spiked with three different (4.14, 7.24, and 10.34 µM) GENTA concentrations, and recorded absorption and FL intensity of hybrid nanosensor.

Chicken egg samples preparation: The practicality of the purposed system was tested in the presence of an egg samples. For the sample preparation taken 15 mg of egg white part and then added 15 mL of DI into it and then stirred for 15 min. After that egg samples extraction was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and collected the supernatant and diluted 50-FD for further experimental test. The absorptions and FL intensity spectra of the hybrid nanosensor were recorded in the presence of 50-FD egg samples. When no trace of GENTA was found in the initial assessment then the egg samples were spiked with the standard 10.34 μM concentration of GENTA and similarly recorded the absorption and FL intensity spectra as do with real samples.

Particle size and surface morphology of AuNPs and GQDs were done by using the TEM analysis technique. Fig. 1 shows TEM and HR-TEM images of GQDs and AuNPs. The particle size of GQDs is uniformly dispersed with relatively narrow size distribution ranging from 1.5 to 5.4 nm as shown in Fig. 1(A). The inset of Fig. 1(A) is represented histogram of particle size having average particle size of 3.37 ± 0.61 nm. Inset of Fig. 1(B) represented the HR-TEM image of GQDs, indicates that large number of GQDs were uniformly arranged with good crystallinity having an interplanar lattice fringe distance of 2.31 Å (Li, Zhou, Li, Li, Zhu, Fan, et al., 2017). The TEM image of AuNPs shows the monodispersed spherical shape AuNPs with an average size of 15.44 ± 1.64 nm as represented in Fig. 1(C). The inset of Fig. 1(D) shows HR-TEM image of AuNPs, depicted the high crystalline particles with an interplanar distance of 2.43 Å corresponds to Au (111) plane (Sajwan, Lakshmi, & Solanki, 2020).

Further, the FTIR was used to analyze the characteristics of functional groups attached to the surface of GQDs and AuNPs shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary data). The FTIR spectrum of AuNPs (curve a) shows the OH stretching characteristic absorbance IR peak around 3379 cm−1. The presence of the carboxylic COO– group on the outer surface of AuNPs was justified with IR peaks appeared at 1383 and 1568 cm−1 (Sajwan, Lakshmi, & Solanki, 2020). The evidence of the surface functional group of GQDs (curve b) was established by the FTIR spectrum. IR peaks at 2998 and 1395 cm−1 show the CH stretching and COO– groups, respectively, represent in the citric acid (Ramachandran, Sathishkumar, Kothurkar, & Senthilkumar, 2018). The peak at 1645 cm−1 shows the CO symmetric stretching vibration band due to the COOH groups, and the peak at 1555 cm−1 shows the presence of the CN group on surface of GQDs. The multiple-band between 1400 and 1600 cm−1 indicates CC stretching in sp2 hybridized compound (Mehta et al., 2017). The IR peaks at 1281 and 3462 cm−1show the presence of the CO and OH stretching vibration bands, respectively.

UV–Vis and FL spectroscopy techniques characterized the optical properties of synthesized GQDs and AuNPs. Fig. 2 (A) shows the absorption spectrum of GQDs as well as AuNPs. Curve (a) indicating a prominent peak at 345 nm ascribing due to the n-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domain corresponding to CO band similar to the graphene oxide (Kundu & Pillai, 2019; Mehrzad-Samarin, Faridbod, Dezfuli, & Ganjali, 2017). The FL emission spectrum of GQDs shows the bright blue emission centered at 416 nm at the 345 nm of excitation wavelength. The inset of Fig. 2(A) provides an image of GQDs in solution when illuminated under UV light. The absorption spectra of AuNPs shows a signature peak near 524 nm (Sajwan, Lakshmi, & Solanki, 2020) and promisingly overlapped with FL emission spectra of GQDs, which satisfied the criteria of the development of the IFE system.

Upon different excitation wavelength varies from 300 to 390 nm, the bright blue emission of GQDs centered at 416 nm as represented in Fig. 2(B). The maximum FL intensity of GQDs was observed when it excited at 345 nm excitation wavelength. It was noted that the FL emission peak at 416 nm is not changed with different excitation, which describes the excitation-independent FL properties of GQDs. The excitation wavelength-independent behavior of GQDs correlated with surface topology and uniform particle size distribution (shown in TEM image) which reduced the surface defects (Dong, Shao, Chen, Li, Wang, Chi, et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of AuNPs concentration on fluorescence intensity of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this IFE donor–acceptor-based sensing platform, GQDs serves as FL donor and AuNPs as FL acceptor. With advantageous to their high extinction coefficient and board absorption spectra in the visible region of AuNPs were widely used as the FL acceptor (quencher) (Mohan, Praveen, Chennazhi, Jayakumar, & Nair, 2013). Fig. 3 (A) shows the emission spectra of GQDs promisingly overlapped with absorption spectra of AuNPs and formed an effective IFE pairs. In IFE system, acceptor (AuNPs) can efficiently absorb the emission intensity of donor (GQDs) when excited at 345 nm wavelength. For the evidence of IFE, the FL decay life-time of GQDs in the presence and absence of AuNPs was investigated with a life-time measurement instrument as depicted in Fig. 3(B). The FL lifet-ime of GQDs was calculated as 9.18 ± 0.03 ns, and 9.16 ± 0.19 ns in the absence and presence of AuNPs, respectively. The life-time of GQDs was hardly changed in the presence of AuNPs, which confirmed that due to the IFE of AuNPs the FL intensity of GQDs quenched along with the increasing concentration of AuNPs.

The FL emission spectra of GQDs in the presence of varying concentrations of AuNPs were recorded and shows in Fig. 3(C). It was observed that the FL emission intensity of GQDs decreases gradually up to 68% with raising concentration of AuNPs, demonstrating the emission intensity of GQDs (donor) sufficiently absorbed by AuNPs (acceptor) due to the IFE. The following Stern-Volmer equation evaluated the value of the quenching constant (Ksv).
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
Q

The FL emission intensity of GQDs in the presence and absence of AuNPs was denoted by F and F0, respectively, KSV is the quenching constant, and AuNPs concentration is denoted by [Q]. The Stern-Volmer plot of quenching (F0/F) of GQDs in the presence of different concentrations of AuNPs varies from 1.02 to 12.86 nM, as shown in Fig. 3(D). It was observed from the Stern-Volmer Eq. (1), which was fitted as:
F 0
F
= 1 + 0.135
A u N P s
n M

The quenching constant KSV was calculated from the slope of the linear plot equals 1.35 × 108 M−1. The higher value of KSV shows the strong interaction between GQDs and AuNPs. Due to the IFE of AuNPs, the FL emission intensity of GQDs was absorbed by AuNPs and hence resulted in the quenching of FL intensity of GQDs.

The effect of GENTA on hybrid nanosensor has been investigated. The proposed sensing system containing AuNPs and fluorescent GQDs in which GQDs serve as a fluorescent donor and the AuNPs shows dual nature as colorimetric receptor and FL acceptor. The sensing phenomenon behind the proposed hybrid nanosensor is dependent on the distance and IFE between the molecules, which change the color of AuNPs and decreased the FL intensity of GQDs, respectively. Fig. 4 (A) represented the FL spectra of (a) GQDs, (b) GQDs + GENTA, (c) GQDs + AuNPs, (d) GQDs + AuNPs + GENTA (5.17 μM) and (e) GQDs + AuNPs + GENTA (10.34 μM) with the same value of experimental parameters. It was clearly shown in Fig. 4(A) that GQDs exhibit a blue FL center at 416 nm when excited at 345 nm. When GENTA was added to the GQDs, the FL intensity GQDs and their peak position is not changed (shown in curve a, b), indicating no interaction between GENTA and GQDs. In the presence of AuNPs, IFE of AuNPs takes place, which significantly decreases the FL intensity of GQDs due to the absorption of emission intensity of GQDs.

However, upon the addition of GENTA into AuNPs, GENTA molecules interacted with AuNPs through the Au-N bond and initiating the aggregation of AuNPs. Due to the aggregation, the IFE between GQDs and AuNPs decreased, turning on the FL intensity of GQDs as represented (curve (d, and e)) in Fig. 4(A). The UV–Vis spectra of AuNPs (a) in the presence of GENTA (b) in Fig. 4(B) show that AuNPs get aggregated in the presence of GENTA, and a new absorption peak has appeared at 678 nm. Due to the agglomeration, the particle size of AuNPs increased up to 22.49 nm (shown in Fig. 4(C), and its color changes from wine red to purple-blue along with the increasing concentration of GENTA as shown in Fig. 4(D). The color-change and FL intensity recovery of nanosensor in the presence of GENTA can be purposed as a simple colorimetric and fluorometric hybrid nanosensor for the detection of GENTA.

Scheme 1 represents the colorimetric and fluorometric determination of GENTA based on distance-dependent and IFE phenomena. GQDs act as an FL energy donor in the developed hybrid nanosensor, and GENTA can act as an aggregation regent for AuNPs. In the presence of AuNPs, the FL emission intensity of GQDs gets absorbed by AuNPs due to the IFE and hence decreased the FL intensity of GQDs. In the IFE system, the emission intensity of GQDs (donor) absorbed by AuNPs (acceptor) resulted in the FL intensity of GQDs quenched in the presence of acceptor (AuNPs) molecules as shown in (curve b) Scheme 1.

When GENTA molecules are added to AuNPs before GQDs, it gets attached to the surface of AuNPs due to the presence of the aliphatic NH2 group on the molecular structure and initiating their aggregation and hence, the color of AuNPs changes from wine red to purple-blue, as shown in Scheme 1. As N atom is highly electron-rich, having a strong binding affinity to electron-deficient Au metal NPs. GENTA is attached to the surface of AuNPs via Au-N bond, and the bond strength is comparable to Au-S bond strength (Sajwan, Lakshmi, & Solanki, 2020). Due to the aggregation AuNPs settle down after the centrifugation, and reduced the IFE of AuNPs resulted in the enhancement in the FL intensity of GQDs. When numerous GENTA molecules get engaged onto the surface of AuNPs, the color of AuNPs turns deeper blue along with the increasing concentration of GENTA as shown in Fig. 4(D) and reduced the IFE of AuNPs on GQDs. As a result, the recovery efficiency of the nanosensor increased and turned on the FL intensity of the developed hybrid nanosensor, as shown in (curve c) Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental condition for the determination of GENTA
ce:section.ce:para: To further improve the performance of hybrid nanosensor to detect GENTA, the effect of critical parameters such as pH value, the concentrations of AuNPs and incubation time have been thoroughly investigated. The FL recovery of the developed nanosensor was increased with the varying concentration of GENTA.

In the recovery process, the FL intensity of the IFE-based nanosensor gradually enhanced with the different GENTA concentration, indicating that the IFE of AuNPs reduced in the presence of GENTA due to the aggregations. The recorded FL recovery efficiency (E) was calculated using the formula mention below with a correction factor α, which is set to normalize and eliminate the effect of different units of FL intensity.
(2)
α =
F
F 0
- F

F and F0 are the FL emission intensity of GQDs with AuNPs and GQDs, respectively.
(3)
Recovery
E f f i c i e n c y
E
= α ∗
F
recovery
- F
F
∗ 100 %
Where,
F
recovery
and
F
are the FL emission intensity of GQDs with GENTA and GQDs, respectively.

Fig. S2(A) represented the influence of pH on the FL recovery efficiency of the nanosensor. It was clearly shown that the FL recovery efficiency increased with pH varying from 2.0 to 6.5 and then dramatically decreased caused by the protonation deprotonation of the carboxylic and amino group present on the surface of GQDs (Song, Quan, Xu, Liu, Cui, & Liu, 2016). Besides this, concentrations of AuNPs also influence the FL recovery efficiency of nanosensor, as depicted in Fig. S2 (B). The recovery efficiency of GQDs was proportionally raised up with AuNPs concentration up to 8.0 nM, and beyond it, the recovery efficiency goes in the opposite trend. As long as the concentration of AuNPs increased, the number of particles increased in the solution compared to GENTA molecules. This extra amount of AuNPs would not be sufficiently protected by the GENTA molecules and IFE is not eliminated properly. Thus the FL intensity of GQDs again starts decreasing in the presence of a higher concentration of AuNPs.

Fig. S2(C and D) represented FL intensity, and recovery efficiency of GQDs increased along the incubation time between GENTA and GQDs-AuNPs nanosensor, respectively. Fig. S2(C and D) represented the recovery efficiency of the system obtained after 10, 20, and 30 min incubation followed by centrifugation at 5400 rpm for 2 min, and showed maximum recovery after 30 min of incubation. Due to the centrifugation, the agglomerated AuNPs settle down. More and more GQDs were released in the solution, which gradually increased the FL intensity and recovery efficiency of GQDs-AuNPs nanosensor shown in Fig. S2(C and D), respectively. Although there is not much difference found in the recovery rate after 20 and 30 min of incubation still recovery is more after 30 min of incubation so 30 min incubation time has been chosen and maintained throughout the experiments. Hence, after the successful evaluation of experimental parameters for the detection of GENTA, it has been concluded that the sensing of GENTA was carried out at a pH value of 6.5 with 8.0 nM AuNPs concentration followed by the 2 min centrifugation after the 30 min of incubation. The value of optimized parameters was kept the same for all future analysis.

The optimized experimental parameters, the colorimetric and fluorometric detection of different GENTA concentrations are presented in Fig. 5 . The AuNPs gets agglomerated in the presence of a varying concentration of GENTA, and their color change from wine red to purple-blue, which can be visualized by the naked eye as shown in Fig. 4(D) and also be quantified by the UV–Vis and FL spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 5(A, C). With the different concentrations of GENTA, AuNPs start aggregation, and a new absorption peak appeared at 678 nm, which confirms the aggregation state of AuNPs (Gukowsky, Tan, Han, & He, 2018). Fig. 5(A) depicted that the absorption peak intensity of AuNPs at 678 nm and 524 nm is increased and decreased, respectively, with increasing GENTA concentration. As a result, the ratio of absorbance between 678 nm (A678) to 524 nm (A524) was adopted to describe the color change caused by varying concentrations of GENTA. A small value of A678/A524 is coupled with the monodispersed AuNPs of red color. While, the larger value is indicated the aggregation of AuNPs, as shown in the colorimetric assays in Fig. 4(D). The value of A678/A524 increased gradually along with the varying concentration of GENTA from 1.03 to 16.55 μM. The inset of Fig. 5(B) indicates that the ratio of A678/A524 shows the linear response with increasing concentration of GENTA with a lower detection limit (LOD) of 0.422 μM. As mentioned, in the presence of GENTA, AuNPs get agglomerated due to the strong binding of GENTA with AuNPs. After the centrifugation, the larger AuNPs settle down and reduced the IFE of AuNPs on the FL intensity of GQDs, and hence the FL intensity on GQDs gradually increased along with the concentration of GENTA varies from 1.03 to 16.55 μM as shown in Fig. 5(C). It was observed that in the presence of GENTA, GENTA molecules bind on the surface of AuNPs and AuNPs started agglomeration, which can significantly reduce the IFE of AuNPs on the FL intensity of GQDs. The conjunction with this, the FL intensity of GQDs increased along with the increasing concentration of GENTA. The linear relationship between the recovery efficiency and varying GENTA concentration is shown in the inset of Fig. 5(D). The nanosensor shows the 95.74% recovery efficiency and linearity within the concentration range of 1.03 to 16 μM with LOD of 0.493 μM.

Table S1 summarized the comparison between the previously reported and the present work for the quantitative analysis of GENTA. The dynamic range and LOD of the purposed hybrid nanosensor were obtained as good and analogous to other reported methods. The proposed hybrid nanosensor showcases their simple and adorable sensing mechanism. In contrast, prepared nanoparticles used without any surface modification led to reduced hard work and increased the cost efficiency of the synthesis. According to the results mentioned in Table S1, the GQDs-AuNPs nanosensor is used as a unique system that possibly will satisfy all the requirements like sensitive, selective, simple, and cost-effective detection of GENTA. The advantages of the developed system were summarized in Table S2.

To check the applicability of the developed hybrid nanosensor, the absorbance of AuNPs and FL intensity of the IFE system was analyzed against the various commonly presented interfering substances present in milk samples. Fig. 6 (A, B) clearly showed that the maximum color change and FL intensity recovery of AuNPs and the IFE system were obtained with GENTA. While carrying out this experiment in different interferents, an optimized concentration of interferents was individually added in AuNPs and checked the color changeability. Fig. S3 shows the visual color and dispersion state with absorption spectra of AuNPs in addition to different interferents. Here, only the presence of GENTA can change the color and absorption peak of AuNPs effectively, as shown in Fig. S3(A–C) and S3 (A1–C1), respectively. The absorbance ratio of A678/A524 and FL recovery efficiency was calculated to validate further the selectivity of the developed hybrid nanosensor towards GENTA, as shown in Fig. 6(A, B). Thus, from the above interference study, it was concluded that the developed hybrid nanosensor gained a high affinity towards GENTA.

For the real sample applicability of the developed hybrid nanosensor, their response was evaluated against the real milk and egg samples and spiked samples. GENTA concentrations in the real and spiked sample have been recorded with colorimetric and fluorometric hybrid nanosensor. The presence of GENTA in the milk and egg samples was measured with the standard experimental procedure under the optimized experimental conditions.

All five different FD diluted milk samples (5-FD, 10-FD, 20-FD, 50-FD, and 100-FD) with 50-FD egg samples were tested for the presence of GENTA by recording the absorbance and emission spectra of hybrid nanosensor in the presence of them. When the GENTA was not found in the milk and egg samples then all the diluted milk and egg samples were spiked with the standard concentration of GENTA represented as 5 FD-SP, 10 FD-SP, 20 FD-SP, 50 FD-SP, and 100 FD-SP milk, and 50 FD-SP egg samples, respectively. The results of the study were presented in Fig. S4 which indicated that the dilution of milk samples plays an important role in the determination of GENTA. The absorbance peak of AuNPs change due to the casein protein and it was found that the AuNPs adhere on the surface of casein protein and the resulting structure have different optical property which protects AuNPs from agglomeration (Gukowsky, Tan, Han, & He, 2018; Liu & Guo, 2009). It was observed that the recovery of the nanosensor increased with the increasing dilution factor from 5-FD to 100-FD and shows maximum recovery with the 100-FD and 50-FD spiked milk and egg samples, respectively.

So that based on the above results further analysis was done with the 100-FD diluted milk sample. For that 100-FD milk sample was spiked with three different standard concentrations (4.14, 7.24, and 10.34 μM) of GENTA and recorded the absorbance ratio (A678/A524) and FL recovery efficiency of the developed nanosensor. Table S3 summarized the diagnostic results for the real sample spiked with a substantial amount of standard concentration of GENTA.

The excellent recovery in colorimetric and fluorometric based detection was calculated with the standard recovery calculation formula (Dai, Shi, Wang, Sun, Hu, Ni, et al., 2014) and the recovery was obtained as 99.36 to 109.73% and 96.24 to 112.12%, respectively. The relative standard deviation of the developed hybrid nanosensor varies as 1.0% to 1.6% and 0.9% to 1.0%, respectively. Based on the above results, it was signifying that the hybrid nanosensor is trustworthy and suitable for real application.

In summary, a new GQDs-AuNPs based hybrid nanosensor was developed with the colorimetric and fluorometric measured method for the quantitative and qualitative determination of GENTA. This proposed nanosensor reflects on its flexibility and ease, during which no further surface modification is mandatory for the prepared NPs, which results in laborious and less expensive synthesis. The readout methods are based on distance-dependent and IFE phenomena, one is a color change of AuNPs in the presence of GENTA, and the second is the absorption of emission intensity of donor due to the IFE. Upon the addition of AuNPs in GQDs, due to the spectral overlapping between them, AuNPs efficiently absorbed the emission intensity of GQDs and hence, decreasing the FL intensity of GQDs. When GENTA was added to AuNPs before GQDs, AuNPs started agglomeration, due to which the AuNPs color changes from red to purple-blue along with the different concentrations of GENTA. The agglomeration of AuNPs in the presence of GENTA reduced the IFE of AuNPs, and the FL intensity of GQDs recovered back along with the GENTA concentration The FL recovery efficiency of GQDs increased up to 95.74%, along with the increasing concentration of GENTA ranging from 1.03 to 16.55 μM with LOD of 0.422 μM and 0.493 μM for colorimetric and fluorometric readout system, respectively. The developed hybrid nanosensor is tested against the spiked milk and egg food samples and shows good recovery efficiency, making the nanosensor attractive for applications to food safety factors.
Reena K. Sajwan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing - Original Draft.
Pratima R. Solanki: Conceived and supervised this study, contributed to revise the manuscript critically and provided fund for this research.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is highly supported by Advanced Instrumentation & Research Facility (AIRF, JNU, New Delhi, for providing a TEM characterization facility. PRS is thankful to the Department of Biotechnology for Indo-Russia (DBT/IC-2/Indo-Russia/2017-19/02), DST Purse, and ICMR, India (34/13/2019-TF/Nano/BMS). The authors are thankful to all funding agencies for providing financial supports and motivation.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.131312.
